coredata.dc:title: Silk fibroin-derived nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots anchored on TiO2 nanotube arrays for heterogeneous photocatalytic degradation and water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qun
Cai, Jingsheng
Biesold-McGee, Gill V.
Huang, Jianying
Ng, Yun Hau
Sun, Hongtao
Wang, Jiping
Lai, Yuekun
Lin, Zhiqun
coredata.dc:description: Bombyx mori silk, a natural nitrogen-rich biopolymer protein, is earth-abundant and sustainable. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) derived from Bombyx mori silk fibroin are immobilized onto TiO2 nanotube arrays (TiO2 NTAs) by a facile hydrothermal process. The resulting N-CQDs decorated TiO2 NTA heterostructures (N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs) display a maximum incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) of 32.5% under 450 nm monochromatic light in neutral solution. Compared with pristine TiO2 NTAs, the N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NTAs demonstrate significantly improved photocatalytic efficiency during the degradation of organic contaminants. Furthermore, the constructed heterostructures are used to split water to investigate their photocatalytic promise, yielding the maximum H2 and O2 production rates of 30.12 and 14.96 μmol cm−2 h−1, respectively. Optimizing the N-CQD coating is found to effectively tune the bandgap and up-conversion capability of the heterostructures, enabling unique photon harvest and boosting photocatalytic activity. This study provides a proof of concept that nonmetal, abundant, and sustainable materials can be exploited to enhance the photocatalytic capability of TiO2, imparting a variety of unique applications such as water purification and chemical fuel production.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image with a SAED pattern (inset), (b) size distribution and (c) HRTEM image of N-CQDs. (d) A representative AFM topography image of N-CQDs on mica (with a height profile plot along the line).

(a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs. Representative SEM images: (b) Typical top-view and (c) side-view images of pristine TiO2 NTAs, (d) top-view and (e) side-view images of prepared N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3.
(a, b) TEM, (c) HRTEM (N-CQDs are in red circles), (d) SAED pattern and (e) corresponding TEM-EDX elemental mappings of prepared N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs.
High-resolution peak-fitting XPS spectra of (a) Ti 2p and (b) O 1s from TiO2 NTAs and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs, (c) C 1s and (d) N 1s from N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs.

(a) LSV curves, (b) I-t curves, (c) EIS Nyquist plots, and (d) IPCE spectra of TiO2 NTAs and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs (measurements performed in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution under simulated solar light irradiation, 0.3 V vs Ag/AgCl, 100 mW cm−2). (e) HER polarization curves and (f) corresponding Tafel plots of N-CQDs, TiO2 NTAs, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 and Pt sheet, respectively, measured in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB and (b) the corresponding kinetic rates by self-degradation, TiO2 NTAs, and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs under simulated solar light irradiation, respectively. (c) Consecutive photocatalytic degradation of MB by N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 for five cycling runs (240 min per cycle). (d) Photocatalytic degradation of phenol and (e) corresponding kinetic rates of self-degradation and various N-CQDs@TiO2 NTA samples under simulated solar light irradiation, respectively. (f) TOC removal efficiency during phenol degradation by TiO2 NTAs and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3, respectively.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of MB after addition of various scavenging agents using N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 under simulated solar light irradiation. ESR spectra recorded at ambient temperature in (b) aqueous dispersion for DMPO-OH· and (c) methanol dispersion for DMPO-O2 – with N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 under solar light irradiation and in dark, respectively.

(a) The evolution rates of H2 and O2 from photocatalytic water splitting using prepared samples under simulated solar light irradiation. The photocatalytic H2 and O2 evolution from water splitting for 5 successive cycling runs (2 h per cycle) by N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 irradiated with (b) full-spectrum, (c) UV and (d) visible light, respectively.

Schematic illustration for mechanism of photocatalytic water splitting over N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs irradiated with solar light, the possible energy band structure and charge separation and transfer pathways.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bombyx mori silk, a natural nitrogen-rich biopolymer protein, is earth-abundant and sustainable. In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) derived from Bombyx mori silk fibroin are immobilized onto TiO2 nanotube arrays (TiO2 NTAs) by a facile hydrothermal process. The resulting N-CQDs decorated TiO2 NTA heterostructures (N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs) display a maximum incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) of 32.5% under 450 nm monochromatic light in neutral solution. Compared with pristine TiO2 NTAs, the N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NTAs demonstrate significantly improved photocatalytic efficiency during the degradation of organic contaminants. Furthermore, the constructed heterostructures are used to split water to investigate their photocatalytic promise, yielding the maximum H2 and O2 production rates of 30.12 and 14.96 μmol cm−2 h−1, respectively. Optimizing the N-CQD coating is found to effectively tune the bandgap and up-conversion capability of the heterostructures, enabling unique photon harvest and boosting photocatalytic activity. This study provides a proof of concept that nonmetal, abundant, and sustainable materials can be exploited to enhance the photocatalytic capability of TiO2, imparting a variety of unique applications such as water purification and chemical fuel production.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of new efficient photocatalysts is critical for a variety of applications including water purification and water splitting. Because of their efficient charge transfer and separation, excellent chemical/photo/thermal-stability, and high specific surface area, various TiO2 nanostructures have been used as photocatalysts for organic pollutant decomposition [1], energy conversion [2], and CO2 reduction [3]. A multitude of one-dimensional TiO2 nanostructures (nanowires [4], nanorods [5], nanofibers [6], and nanotubes [7]) have been studied because the 1D structure provides a continuous channel for the transport of photo-generated electrons and enhanced charge transfer and separation from the reduction of grain boundaries [8]. A two-step electrochemical anodic oxidation process was employed to synthesize highly photocatalytically active TiO2 nanotube arrays (TiO2 NTAs) with highly ordered nanotubular structures and improved specific surface areas [9]. Unfortunately, because TiO2 has a wide band gap, the photocatalytic applications of TiO2 NTAs are restricted to light in the ultraviolet (UV) range. For practical photocatalytic applications a smaller bandgap is needed, and in an effort to achieve that, much work has been done to alter the electronic structure of TiO2 by designing unique nanostructures [10], doping with other species [11], and decorating TiO2 with narrow bandgap semiconductors to construct heterostructures with a photoresponse to visible light [12]. Specifically, constructed semiconductor heterostructures have drawn much attention due to their significant improvement in light utilization, which effectively enhances both photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic activity. It is notable that semiconductor quantum dots and nanosheets as well as single metal atoms have been used as sensitizers due to their easily adjustable band gap edge and quantum confinement effects [13]. A variety of semiconductor quantum dots have been used to sensitize TiO2 NTAs for the utilization of visible light, including metal sulfide [14], metal selenide [15], and metallic telluride [16]. Unfortunately, these semiconductor quantum dots contain toxic metals which restricts their further widespread application as sensitizers.

Because graphitic carbon nitride has a nonmetallic character, visible-light response, high biocompatibility, and a favorable conduction band (CB) for reduction reactions [17], it has been used for the photocatalytic removal of pollutants [18], photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen generation [19], bio-imaging [20], and electrocatalytic reduction of oxygen [21]. When graphitic carbon nitride is used to sensitize TiO2 NTAs, photogenerated electrons from the CB of graphitic carbon nitride can easily transfer to the CB of TiO2 NTAs, which can then move along the nanotubular structures and easily facilitate reduction reactions. Simultaneously, photogenerated holes from the valence band (VB) of TiO2 NTAs can transfer to the VB of graphitic carbon nitride and participate in oxidizing reactions. Additionally, the defects resulting from decorating TiO2 with graphitic carbon nitride can function as unique reactive sites that improve photocatalytic performance. Similar to graphitic carbon nitride, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are intriguing candidates for water redox catalysis due to their carbon-layered morphology, electronic structure, chemical stability, and facile preparation from earth-abundant raw materials. Notably, N-CQDs exhibit beneficial quantum confinement and up-conversion capability, while graphitic carbon nitride does not. Thus, the ability to create intentionally defect rich heterostructures by coupling TiO2 with N-deficient CQDs stands out as an intriguing strategy for markedly boosting photocatalytic activity.

Defect-rich heterostructures possess many advantages, such as plentiful active sites, enhanced redox capability, and improved visible light responsivity. Abundant defects in heterostructures can also promote the contact among different components and alter semiconductor band structure, allowing for the fabrication of defect-mediated heterojunction systems. Relatively small defects from the introduction of carbon nitride into TiO2 heterostructures were observed to enhance photocatalytic activity [22], yet no study has examined the effects of larger of defects from the incorporation of carbon nitride quantum dots. Because synthesis of high quantum yield N-CQDs has remained a challenge [23], the exploration of facile and efficient methods to produce novel photoluminescent N-CQDs with uniform morphologies and high quantum yields is of great importance.

Carbonaceous nanomaterials are often synthesized from biomass materials which are relatively inexpensive, nontoxic, and abundant [24]. Cocoon silk (generated from Bombyx mori silkworm) is a biomass that has drawn particular interest because it contains silk fibroin, a material that can easily be dissolved for regeneration into various morphologies [25,26]. Herein, we develop a facile strategy to synthesize water-soluble N-CQDs using cocoon silk as the precursor created via additive-free dissolution and hydrothermal processes. The resultant N-CQDs are smaller than 10 nm and possess: uniform morphologies, plentiful nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups, and unique fluorescent properties. Furthermore, the morphology, size and structure of N-CQDs can be tailored by controlling silk fibroin contribution, hydrothermal temperature, hydrothermal time, and post-treatment process. These fluorescent N-CQDs will then be hydrothermally combined with TiO2 NTAs to yield defect-rich heterostructures. Notably, the interfaces within this heterostructure are intimately contacted, which facilitates facile charge transfer and separation at the active sites. This defect-rich system displays expanded solar light utilization and significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability.

We also note that although this heterostructure is superior to most traditional catalysts, many challenges still remain for its practical applications. First, more scalable and controllable synthetic methods are required to be developed for producing N-CQDs for large-scale applications. Second, the N-CQDs may be easily agglomerated due to high surface energy, making it difficult maintain their dispersion and stability on TiO2 NTAs. Third, the interaction between N-CQDs and TiO2 NTAs plays a significant role in achieving the high efficient photocatalytic activity, thus the study on understanding how N-CQDs interact with TiO2 NTAs for promoting the photocatalytic capability is needed.

A typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of N-CQDs prepared with 3 wt% silk fibroin solution (Fig. 1 a) reveals that N-CQDs are uniform in size with an average diameter around 7.5 nm. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 1b) indicates that the N-CQDs are monodisperse, with a uniform dynamic size ranging from around 4 to 10 nm. High resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1c) reveal that while lattice fringes are not obvious on most N-CQDs, some N-CQDs have discernible lattice fringes, which indicates both a crystalline and amorphous nature. Polycrystalline nature of the prepared N-CQDs is also verified by the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset of Fig. 1a). The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of N-CQDs (Fig. 1d) suggests that the prepared N-CQDs are well-dispersed in water. The typical heights ranging from 1.5 to 3 nm are observed from a cross-sectional topography (inset of Fig. 1d), indicating that N-CQDs consist of several layers of C–N sheets. In general, topographic heights of hydrothermally synthesized graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots are in the range between 0.3 and 2.1 nm [27].

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of prepared N-CQDs (Fig. S1a displays two obvious peaks centered at 22.34° (signed with ★) and 26.27°, corresponding to amorphous carbon and the (002) planes of graphitic carbon, respectively [28]. Thus, it can be seen that N-CQDs contain both amorphous and crystalline phases of carbon, which agrees well with the HRTEM observation. Moreover, it is also confirmed that carbonizing silk fibroin can produce graphitic structures. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) was done to confirm the surface functional groups of N-CQDs. As shown in the FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. S1b), characteristic peaks observed at 3440 and 3186 cm−1 are ascribed to the –OH and N–H stretching vibration, respectively, revealing that N-CQDs contain abundant hydroxyl and amino groups. The characteristic peaks found at 1235, 1065 and 850 cm−1 are attributed to C–O vibration, C–O–C stretching vibration and N–H deformation vibration, respectively. These functional groups give the outstanding solubility of N-CQDs [29]. The zeta potential of N-CQDs in aqueous solution was found to be −35.6 eV, indicating that carboxyl functional groups with negative charge exist on the N-CQDs’ surface. The chemical structure, phase, and crystallinity quality of N-CQDs materials can be analyzed by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in the Raman of N-CQDs (Fig. S1c), two major peaks centered at 1349 and 1600 cm−1 are ascribed to the amorphous D and crystalline G band of carbon, respectively [30]. The ratio of intensities of the D band to G band (I D/I G) is 0.89, which is similar to previously reported high crystallinity quality graphene quantum dots [30a].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to study valence states and elemental compositions of prepared samples. The full scanned XPS spectrum of prepared N-CQDs (Fig. S2a) shows three main peaks at 284, 399 and 532 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. Moreover, the high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. S2b) shows characteristic peaks at 284.7, 285.6, 286.4 and 288.0 eV, ascribing to C–C/CC, C–N, C–O and CO/CN, respectively [29b]. The deconvolution of N 1s spectrum (Fig. S2c) shows characteristic peaks at 399.7 eV, 400.2 eV and 401.3 eV, ascribing to three relative nitrogen species of C–N–C, N–C3 and N–H, respectively [ [24b,31]]. Specifically, the C–N–C from N-CQDs shows slightly higher binding energy compared to that of layered C3N4 (usually 388.5–397.6 eV), which may result from plentiful functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl etc.) with strong electronegativity bound to the as-synthesized N-CQDs surface. These electronegative functional groups can distort the distribution of electrons on materials surface, resulting higher binding energies [22b]. The deconvolution of the O 1s spectrum (Fig. S2d) presents two characteristic peaks centered at 530.2 and 531.5 eV, ascribing to –OH and CO, respectively [29b]. Additionally, the result of element analysis (Table S1) confirms that the N-CQDs consist of C (59.25%), H (4.86%), N (13.21%) and O (22.68%) atoms, which is consistent with the XPS investigation. When compared to cocoon silk, the samples have a higher carbon content and a lower oxygen content, which suggests the rupture of molecular chains and sufficient carbonization after the dissolution and hydrothermal reaction. Therefore, it is concluded that the synthesized N-CQDs mainly consist of aromatic carbon and nitrogen species derived from amino acids’ dehydration and polymerization, resulting in plentiful hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and amino functional groups on their surface.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were employed to study the optical properties of N-CQDs in aqueous solution. The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S3a) of N-CQDs shows two absorption peaks at 275 and 330 nm, typically ascribing to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and n-π* transition of CO band, respectively [ [29b,32]]. By plotting the square root of absorption energy (αhν, where α and hν are the absorbance and photon energy, respectively) against hν (inset in Fig. S3a), the bandgap energy of N-CQDs is determined to be 1.92 eV, which is remarkably narrower than the 3.60 eV bandgap of undoped CQDs (Fig. S4). This result demonstrates that N doping can effectively reduce the bandgap of CQDs. It is notable that the bandgap of synthesized CQDs is similar to the reported bandgap of carbon nanodots [30b]. From photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and PL spectra of N-CQDs (Fig. S3b), it can be seen that the strongest emission peak centers at 420 nm under 330nm UV light excitation. As illustrated by the inset in Fig. S3a, the N-CQDs give off a strong blue fluorescence when irradiated with 365 nm UV light, and appear slightly yellow under visible light. Moreover, the PL spectra of N-CQDs excited by various excitation wavelengths ranging from 290 to 450 nm show an excitation-dependent emission trend, which is consistent with previously reported CQDs [29b,29c,31,33]. As depicted in Fig. S3c, the PL peak is blue-shifted from 425 to 415 nm and increased in peak intensity when the excitation wavelength increases from 290 to 330 nm, but shows a red shift from 430 to 510 nm and decrease in peak intensity as the excitation wavelength increases from 350 to 450 nm. The excitation dependent emission in longer wavelength regions may be ascribed to sp2 hybridization of carbon clusters with size effect [29b,34]. The maximum fluorescence peak of N-CQDs at 420 nm and the PL quantum yield of 38% are comparable to those in previous literatures [35], but higher than that reported for carbon dots synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of orange juice [36]. This may be ascribed to the existence of various amine groups, and that is perhaps owing to amid-containing fluorophores [37]. The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) of N-CQDs (Fig. S3d) can be fitted well using a biexponential function, showing an average carrier lifetime of 1.94 ns (under excitation of 370 nm). The observed nanosecond lifetime of N-CQDs suggests that N-CQDs emission can be attributed to a singlet state. Additionally, along with increasing excitation wavelengths at 650, 700, 750, 800, 850 and 900 nm, the N-CQDs show upconverted emission spectra (Fig. S5). This up-conversion property implied that the N-CQDs have promising applications for photocatalyst design, photovoltaic device, and light energy conversion [32b].

The heterostructure constructed by loading N-CQDs onto TiO2 NTAs was obtained via a two-step anodization followed by hydrothermal method as illustrated in Fig. 2 a. N-CQDs obtained from the hydrothermal treatment of 2, 3, 5 and 7 wt% silk fibroin solution are applied to decorate TiO2 NTAs, respectively, and the corresponding samples are signaled as N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-2, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-5, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-7. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was employed to observe the surface morphology of the N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs heterostructures. The SEM images of pristine TiO2 NTAs (Fig. 2b and c) show that these nanotubular structures with uniform nanorings are regularly arranged, with lengths and average pore diameters around 1.0 μm and 100 nm, respectively. These uniform nano-rings can facilitate electron transfer on TiO2 surface, thereby enhancing the photoelectrochemical activity of TiO2 NTAs [22a,38]. A tunable amount and distribution of N-CQDs can be anchored on the TiO2 NTAs simply by changing the silk fibroin concentration. Samples made using 3 wt% silk fibroin solution seemed to be the best, with N-CQDs well-dispersed on the TiO2 NTAs surface and no blocked channels (Fig. 2d and e). Samples made with 2 wt% silk fibroin solution saw a small amount of N-CQDs anchored on TiO2 NTAs (Fig. S6a and b), samples made with 5 wt% silk fibroin (Fig. S6c and d) had part of nanotubes channels were blocked by aggregated N-CQDs, and using 7 wt% silk fibroin resulted in that most nanotubes were covered by massive aggregated N-CQDs (Fig. S6e and f). It is a remarkable fact that TiO2 NTAs still show ordered nanotubular structures after hydrothermal process, suggesting a facile strategy for preparing the heterostructures without damaging nanotubes.

Low magnification TEM images of the typical N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 (Fig. 3 a and b) show that N-CQDs marked with red circles are well-dispersed on nanotubular structures. Lattice fringes of 0.35 nm (corresponding to the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 [22b]) can be observed in the HRTEM image of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs (Fig. 3c). The SAED pattern of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs (Fig. 3d) shows diffraction rings from the inside out that correspond to (101), (110), (004) and (200) fringe planes, indicating the polycrystalline nature of TiO2 [13b,38]. Elemental mapping was done via TEM-EDX (Fig. 3e) to further confirm the homogeneous dispersion of C and N within prepared N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs, suggesting the abundant contact among various components, and giving the visible evidence of the heterojunction of prepared N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs with the deep hybridization. Additionally, the TEM-EDX spectrum (Fig. S7) reveals that the constructed heterostructure contains 7.34 and 1.62 wt% of C and N, respectively. We note that Cu signal in the EDX spectrum is from substrate of copper grid.

The XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs (Fig. S8) confirms the existence of C and N besides Ti and O elements. The deconvolution of Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 4 a) shows two characteristic peaks at 464.3 and 458.6 eV, assigned to the Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2, respectively. The spin-orbital doublet splitting of 5.7 eV reveals Ti4+ existing in anatase TiO2 [39]. The higher locations of Ti 2p1/2 (464.9 eV) and Ti 2p3/2 (459.1 eV) in Ti 2p spectrum for N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs may arise from the decreased electron intensity of the Ti atom due to the strong interfacial interactions [40]. For N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs, the deconvolution of Ti 2p peaks shows a higher energy shift, probably resulting from that the presence of the Ti3+ chemical state [41]. In contrast to pure TiO2 NTAs, the deconvolution of O 1s spectrum for N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs (Fig. 4b) shows the slight shift of Ti–O binding energy to 529.9 eV and a higher intensity of the O–H binding energy at 531.7 eV, indicating more oxygen-containing functional groups exist on the heterostructure surface [39]. For the XPS spectrum of the C 1s signal (Fig. 4c), four characteristic peaks located at 284.7, 285.6, 286.5 and 288.2 eV correspond to C–C, C–N, C–O and CN/CO configurations, respectively [29b]. The XPS spectrum for the N 1s signal (Fig. 4d) shows three peaks after deconvolution at 399.5, 400.1 and 401.3 eV, corresponding to C–N–C、N–C3 and C–N–H groups, respectively [22b]. The XPS spectra of the C 1s and N 1s for N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs are consistent with those for pure N-CQDs (Fig. S2b and c), suggesting the successful deposition of N-CQDs on TiO2 NTAs.

FTIR was used to further study the chemical group compositions contained in prepared photocatalysts. In contrast to pristine TiO2 NTAs, the FTIR spectra of prepared N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs (Fig. S9) show weak characteristic bands at 2957 and 2867 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibrations of CH3 and CH2, respectively [42]. The broad bands between 1000 and 1700 cm−1 centered at 1645, 1405, 1250 and 1076 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the functional groups CC, CH2, C–O and C–O–C, respectively [24b,29c], which are consistent with those obtained from pure N-CQDs. Furthermore, the peak intensities of these carbon-containing groups increase with increasing silk fibroin solution concentration, indicating an increased amount of N-CQDs deposited on TiO2 NTAs with increasing the concentration of silk fibroin solution. Raman spectroscopy serves as further proof of N-CQDs bound to TiO2 NTAs. A sharp characteristic peak found at 145 cm−1 can be found in the Raman spectra of all prepared samples (Fig. S10a) and is ascribed to the Eg vibration modes of anatase TiO2 [43]. The Raman bands centered at 196, 396, 512 and 639 cm−1 correspond to Eg, Bg, A1g and Eg modes of anatase TiO2, respectively [44]. The two Raman bands located at 1601 and 1345 cm−1 for N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs are assigned to G and D bands of carbon, respectively [45]. The intensities of these G and D bands increases with increasing silk fibroin solution concentration, indicating that the amount of N-CQDs on TiO2 NTAs increases with the precursor concentration. XRD was used to confirm the phase purity and crystallinity of prepared samples. As depicted in the XRD patterns of all prepared samples (Fig. S10b), diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 25.6°, 37.8°, 48.4°, 53.8°, 55.3°, 62.9° and 69.2° are well corresponded to (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204) and (116) crystal planes of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21–1272) [38]. An additional peak with a 2θ value of 26.3° exists for all samples with N-CQDs and can be assigned to the (002) plane of amorphous carbon is present in N-CQDs [44]. Furthermore, the peak at 26.3° increases in intensity as silk fibroin concentration increases, further suggesting increased N-CQD deposition. The peak intensity of the (002) plane of amorphous carbon is very weak, which may be attributed to the extreme diffraction peaks intensity of Ti substrate and small amount of N-CQDs dispersed on TiO2 NTAs surface.

Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were conducted to accurately examine the content of N-CQDs in the hybrid. After heating to 250 °C in air, an initial weight loss of 0.30% was observed, which can be attributed to the removal of surface-adsorbed water (Fig. S11). From 250 to 550 °C, a weight loss of 0.95% was seen and assigned to the combustion of N-CQDs [39]. The experimentally determined 0.95 wt% N-CQD qualitatively agreed well with the SEM and TEM images, displaying few N-CQDs anchored on TiO2 NTAs.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were used to investigate the optical absorption performance of prepared photocatalysts. As shown in the UV–vis DRS of prepared samples (Fig. 5 a), the intense absorbance exhibited in the UV region for pristine TiO2 NTAs may be ascribed to the electronic transition from VB to CB (O 2p→Ti 3d) [42]. It was also found that the light absorption of TiO2 NTAs decorated with N-CQDs increased over the whole visible light range, due to the N-CQDs promoting the harvest of visible light photons. In addition, the visible light absorption shoulder between 400 and 550 nm indicates the successful formation of heterostructures in N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs. N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 exhibits a maximum absorbance over the visible light range among all prepared samples, suggesting that 3 wt% silk fibroin solution served as the optimized precursor for effective N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs heterostructures. This improved visible light absorbance is also used to explain the enhanced photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic activities of prepared N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs, as investigated later. The extended photoresponse wavelength range of prepared heterostructures is attributed to its relatively narrower band gap, which is confirmed by plots of the transformed Kubelka-Munk function ((αhν)
2 ) vs. photon energy (hν). The band gap energy (E
g ) was calculated by the following equation [42]:
(1)
a h v = A
(
h v −
E g
)
n / 2
where A is a constant, α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, and n equals to 1/2 for semiconductors that exhibit direct transitions. As shown in Fig. 5b, the modified TiO2 NTAs exhibit band gap energies of 2.25–3.10 eV, which are smaller than that of pure TiO2 NTAs (3.20 eV). It is worth noting that the photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic activity of semiconductors are enhanced with decreasing their band gap energies. N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 displays the lower band gap energy (2.25 eV), and therefore is expected to have the most improved photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic activities.

PL emission spectrum can be employed to verify the efficiency of charge trapping and recombination of photogenerated carriers in semiconductor. As shown in Fig. 5c, the PL spectra of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs are similar with pristine TiO2 NTAs, indicating the main character of TiO2 in PL generation. Two main emission peaks located at 467 and 598 nm are probably assigned to defect levels induced by oxygen vacancies or surface states formed through annealing process [46]. A weaker PL intensity means less radiative recombination, which correlates to a better separation rate of electron-hole pairs [47]. The PL intensities of prepared N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs are much weaker than that of pristine TiO2 NTAs, suggesting that the heterostructure shows a lower recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. This result mainly results from that the excited electrons can readily transfer between N-CQDs and TiO2, leading to the suppressed recombination of electrons and holes. Moreover, the N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 shows the lowest PL intensity among all prepared samples, suggesting that it is indeed the sample with the most optimal synthesis conditions. TRPL spectroscopy was performed on the prepared samples to elucidate the charge transfer kinetics. In this work, the obtained time resolved emission decay curves monitored at λ
ex = 370 nm were fitted by the following biexponential function [48]:
(2)

)

A 2

)

1 and τ
2 are the short and long lifetimes for exponential components, respectively, and A

(3)

As shown in the fluorescent decay curves of prepared samples (Fig. 5d), the average exciton lifetimes of modified TiO2 NTAs are 5.63, 7.67, 5.02 and 3.37 ns, corresponding to N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-2, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-5 and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-7, respectively, all of which are longer than that of pristine TiO2 NTAs (1.80 ns). This indicates that the N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs heterostructure shows effectively retarded recombination of photoinduced charge carriers and prolonged retention of hot electrons, as well as enhanced photocatalytic activity. N-CQDs with excellent electrical conductivity can provide channels for photoinduced electron transport, and TiO2 NTAs provide an additional energy level for accepting electrons from N-CQDs, resulting in the facilitated injection rate of photoinduced electrons. The prolonged lifetime of electron-holes pairs means the weakened PL emission intensity, which is consistent with PL investigations. Because N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 shows both the maximum average carrier lifetime and the most drastic quenching of photoluminescence, it must possess the maximum carrier separation efficiency.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) tests were used to investigate the photoelectrochemical activity of prepared samples. From the obtained LSV curves (Fig. 6 a), it can be seen that TiO2 NTAs loaded with N-CQDs exhibit a significantly improved photoresponse when compared to pristine TiO2 NTAs. The saturation photocurrent density of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 reaches 0.97 mA cm−2 at 0.3 V vs Ag/AgCl, which is almost 3 times that of pristine TiO2 NTAs (photocurrent density of 0.33 mA cm−2 under the simulate sunlight irradiation). Open circuit potential (VOC) is employed to evaluate the Fermi level difference between work electrode and reference electrode. The VOC of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs is greater than that of pristine TiO2 NTAs, indicating a more negative potential shift in the Fermi level, which is mainly assigned to the fact that the use of N-CQDs as photosensitizer can effectively improve the injection rate of photogenerated electrons [49].

The photocurrent densities versus time curves were collected, using simulated sunlight (with a 30s on/off frequency) at 0.3 V vs Ag/AgCl. From I-t curves shown in Fig. 6b, it can be observed that the prepared samples display photostability after 330 s of photoelectrochemical tests, achieving photocurrent densities of 0.30, 0.63, 0.95, 0.56 and 0.42 mA cm−2, corresponding to pure TiO2 NTAs, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-2, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-5 and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-7, respectively. This result suggests that the enhanced photocurrent density is results from the anchoring of an optimal amount of N-CQDs.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to investigate the process of charge transfer and transport. A smaller semicircle diameter in Nyquist plots obtained from EIS tests suggests a lower charge transfer resistance [22b]. As presented in the Nyquist plots of prepared samples (Fig. 6c), N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs exhibit smaller semicircular diameters than that for pristine TiO2 NTAs under simulate sunlight irradiation, with N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 exhibiting the smallest. Clearly, the arches for N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs both in the dark and under simulate sunlight irradiation are much smaller than those for TiO2 NTAs, implying that introduction with N-CQDs effectively improved the electron transfer by suppressing the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Furthermore, incident photon-to-current conversion efficiencies (IPCE) measurement was performed to determinate the ratio of incident photons that are successfully translated to photocurrent as a function of wavelength. As shown in the IPCE plots (Fig. 6d), the pristine TiO2 NTAs show an intense photoresponse in UV region (300–400 nm), and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 show an additional photoresponse in a portion of the visible light region (400–550 nm). IPCE reaches 6.0% for N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 irradiated with the monochromatic light at 450 nm, while it is nearly negligible for pristine TiO2 NTAs at the same condition. This result confirms that the use of N-CQDs as a photosensitizer can facilitate improved visible light utilization.

For further investigating electrochemical properties, we conducted the electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activities of prepared samples in acidic solution. Fig. 6e presents iR-corrected polarization curves of HER in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution for N-CQDs, TiO2 NTAs, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3, and Pt used as a controlled study. This shows that the N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 display a notably low overpotential of 305 mV at 10 mA cm−2, superior to that of pristine TiO2 NTAs. All reported potentials are against reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). The Tafel slope are employed to measure HER kinetics and can be determined by Tafel plots. The Tafel slope are depicted using the following equation [50]:
(4)
η = b
log
j + a
where η is the overpotential, and a, j and b are the constant, current density and Tafel slope, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6f, the N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 exhibits a Tafel slope of 47.8 mV·dec−1, which is lower than that of pristine TiO2 NTAs (82.0 mV·dec−1). The lower Tafel slope of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs implied its excellent HER performance and the dominant Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism [51]. It is justified that the designed heterostructure can promote the electrochemical activity of TiO2, which is assigned to the strong electronic and chemical coupling between N-CQDs and TiO2.

I-t curves and EIS measurements were performed on prepared samples under UV and visible light irradiation, respectively, to further confirm that the use of photosensitizer N-CQDs is beneficial to TiO2 NTAs for photon harvest. As shown in I-t curves of prepared samples (Fig. S12a), the pristine TiO2 NTAs irradiated with UV light show a photocurrent density of 0.31 mA cm−2, which is comparable to that under full spectral illumination. The UV photocurrent density of prepared N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 is 0.63 mA cm−2, which is lower than that of seen under full spectral irradiation. Furthermore, the prepared N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 irradiated with visible light show significantly higher photocurrent density than that for pure TiO2 NTAs at the same condition: approximately 0.08 mA cm−2 for N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3, while it is nearly negligible for pristine TiO2 NTAs. The Nyquist plots of prepared samples were obtained when using UV and visible light as light sources, respectively. As shown in Fig. S12b, the N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 exhibits a smaller semicircular diameter than that for pristine TiO2 NTAs under UV and visible light irradiation, respectively, indicating it always has superior charge transfer properties. Both the I-t and EIS tests demonstrate that N-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NTAs consistently have better photocurrent and charge transfer over all light irradiation.

The photocatalytic degradation of MB and phenol was used to evaluate the photocatalytic capability of the synthesized heterostructures. In general, the photocatalytic degradation rate corresponds to the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model [52]:

Concerning the photocatalytic removal of MB under simulated sunlight irradiation (Fig. 7 a), it can be seen that almost no MB was degraded after 240 min in the absence of photocatalysts, but distinct MB degradation can be observed when adding photocatalysts. For pure TiO2 NTAs, 15.8% of MB was degraded within 240 min, while N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs composites present promoted photocatalytic removal efficiency. The MB removal efficiencies over the same timeline reach 57.2%, 61.5%, 40.3% and 25.2%, corresponding to N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-2, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-5 and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-7, respectively. The photocatalytic efficiency increases along with the increase of N-CQDs amount within a certain range, while further increase of N-CQDs amount beyond that range will lead to a decrease of photocatalytic efficiency. This decreased photocatalytic activity is probably caused by the self-aggregation of excess N-CQDs on TiO2 NTAs prevents the absorption of incident light by nanotubes and restrains the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Fig. 7b depicts the kinetic constant of MB degradation by various prepared samples. The optimum photocatalytic efficiency is obtained with N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 (0.0041 min−1), which is around 5.9 times higher than that of pure TiO2 NTAs. These results suggest that the decoration of N-CQDs onto TiO2 NTAs can boost photocatalytic activity and that an optimal mass will result in peak photocatalytic performance.

The repeatability and stability of photocatalysts are of great importance to actual photocatalytic applications. The photocatalytic repeatability of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 was investigated via a cycling test with 240 min per cycle. As plotted in Fig. 7c, the degradation efficiency of MB decreased from 61.5% to 58.4% after five consecutive cycling runs, suggesting that the photocatalytic ability of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 was slightly decreased. Because of the small nature of this activity decrease, this test confirms that N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs show excellent stability under sunlight irradiation. Total organic carbon (TOC) removal measurements were conducted on organic pollutants to study the mineralization ability of photocatalysts. TOC removal efficiencies were measured using N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 as repeated photocatalysts for five successive cycling runs of MB degradation under simulated sunlight illumination. As shown in Fig. S13, the N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 shows a TOC removal of 42.9% for the first cycle with 240 min and 39.5% after five repeated cycles. This small drop in removal ability suggests that N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs is highly stable and can effectively mineralize MB under sunlight irradiation.

Phenol molecules were also employed as a simulated pollutant to further evaluate the heterostructure's photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 7d, the N-CQDs@TiO2 NTA composites demonstrated superior photocatalytic capability compared to pure N-CQDs or TiO2 NTAs. The amount of N-CQDs anchored on the TiO2 NTAs was found to significantly influence photocatalytic performance. Pristine TiO2 NTAs achieved only a 14.6% phenol removal efficiency after 320 min under simulated solar light irradiation. Moderate N-CQD decoration onto the NTAs was found to result in a remarkable increase in photocatalytic efficiency toward the degradation of phenol. Among all of the prepared hybrids, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 exhibited the best photocatalytic performance, with a 61.4% phenol removal efficiency in 320 min. The corresponding photodegradation kinetic rates for phenol removal are depicted in Fig. 7e. Notably, the trend for phenol degradation is consistent with that observed for MB degradation. The ability of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs to effectively degrade phenol molecules (which are relatively more difficult to decompose than MB) further suggested that they are excellent candidates for the photodegradation of a variety of organic contaminants.

N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs also demonstrated excellent photocatalytic stability. After 10 cycles, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 maintained a phenol removal efficiency of 57.1%, only slightly less than the 61.4% of the initial cycling run (Fig. S14). The morphology and structural property of prepared samples after 10 cycles of phenol photodegradation were investigated to further confirm this stability. The representative SEM images (Fig. S15a and b) show that these nanotubular structures loaded with N-CQDs are still regularly arranged, almost without damage from consecutive illumination. Moreover, the representative TEM image shows that N-CQDs are well-dispersed on TiO2 NTAs, and lattice fringes of TiO2 (0.35 nm) can be observed from the HRTEM image of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 used as photocatalysts for successive degradation of phenol for 10 cycles (Fig. S15c and d). Obviously, there is almost no difference of diffraction peaks in XRD patterns of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 before and after consecutive photocatalytic degradation of phenol for 10 cycles (Fig. S16). Additionally, the photodegradation of phenol by prepared samples was conducted under visible light (λ ˃ 420 nm) irradiation. As can be seen from Fig. S17, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 achieved a phenol removal efficiency of 22.4% under visible light (after 360 min), yet nearly no phenol removal was observed when using pristine TiO2 NTAs under the same conditions. This suggests that the hybrid samples effectively boosted visible light harvest for TiO2 NTAs after optimum N-CQDs anchoring.

Moreover, the efficient TOC removal of phenol was also realized with TiO2 NTAs (and its hybrids), signifying the effective mineralization of phenol by the as-prepared photocatalyst samples. As shown in Fig. 7f, TOC removal efficiency increases with time. The addition of N-CQDs also led to significantly improved TOC removal efficiencies, with raw TiO2 and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 achieving 12.5% and 49.3%, respectively (320 min of irradiation).

An in-situ capture techniques were conducted to determine the main active species during the photocatalytic degradation of MB. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the addition of ethylene diamine tetra-acetic acid (EDTA, a typical holes scavenger) only slightly suppressed MB removal (falling to 57.9%), which suggests that holes have only a minor role as an oxidizing agent during the photocatalytic process. Conversely, the addition of 1 mM tert-butanol (t-BuOH, a typical quencher for OH·) suppressed MB removal to only 24.9% (after 240 min of photocatalytic process), which indicates that OH· is the main oxidant involved in the organic photodegradation process. For comparison, the photodegradation was moderately suppressed to about 50.7% MB removal after the addition of benzoquinone (BQ, a typical scavenger for O2 −), indicating that O2 − is also a primary active species during the organic photodegradation process. Photodegradation was also suppressed to about 41.3% MB removal when β-carotene (a typical single oxygen scavenger) was introduced in the photocatalytic process, revealing the significant role of single oxygen during the photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants. The ESR spin-trap technique was used to detect photoinduced reactive oxygen species during the photocatalytic process. Four characteristic peaks with intensity 1:2:2:1 for DMPO-OH· appeared under simulated solar light irradiation, but no ESR signals were observed in dark (Fig. 8b). This result suggests that OH· was produced at the surface of the N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs by the oxidization of H2O/OH−. Additionally, the ESR spectrum of DMPO-O2 - (Fig. 8c) shows four characteristic peaks with intensity 1:1:1:1 under simulated solar light irradiation but no signals in the dark, signifying that O2 − is effectively generated over N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs through the reduction of dissolved O2. The findings of these ESR tests further substantiated that OH· and O2 − are major active species in the photocatalytic degradation of MB and phenol by N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs.

As a superior photocatalyst, the VB and CB located at suitable positions play curial roles during photocatalytic degradation process. For confirming photogenerated charge carriers separation mechanism, the valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VB-XPS) was employed to determine the energy band structures of TiO2 and N-CQDs. As shown in Figure

S18a and b, the VB bandgap (EVB) maximums relative to the Fermi level are determined to be 2.72 and 1.18 eV for TiO2 and N-CQDs, respectively. Since the bandgaps of TiO2 and N-CQDs obtained from the UV–vis DRS curve are 3.20 and 1.92 eV (Fig. 5b and inset in Fig. S3a), respectively, the CB bandgap (ECB) of TiO2 and N-CQDs can be estimated to be −0.48 and −0.74 eV, respectively, according to the formula ECB = EVB - Eg. This result corresponds to the CB bandgap acquired from the Mott-Schottky plots. According to the Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. S18 c and d), the flat-band potentials (EFB) are determined to be −0.72 and −0.93 eV (vs AgCl) for TiO2 and N-CQDs, respectively. Corrected by AgCl (vs 0.20 eV), the ECB of TiO2 and N-CQDs is calculated to be −0.52 and −0.73 eV (vs NHE), respectively [53]. In addition, the energy band structure of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs was investigated to further demonstrate the narrow band gap of this heterostructure. The EVB maximum and EFB of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs are measured to be 1.70 and −0.78 eV, respectively (Fig. S19a and b). Its ECB occurs at about −0.58 eV (vs NHE). This result provides strong evidence that optimum N-CQDs loading effectively leads to the narrow bandgap of the heterostructure.

During the photocatalytic process, electrons and holes can generate from the CB and VB of N-CQDs and TiO2, respectively. Due to the CB position of the N-CQDs is less positive than that of TiO2, the photoinduced electrons from N-CQDs can transfer to the CB of TiO2. The electrons accumulated on TiO2 react with oxygen molecules to produce O2 − that can further oxidize H2O to yield H2O2 and OH·. In addition, the holes generated from the VB of the TiO2 can quickly transfer to N-CQDs due to the VB position of TiO2 is more positive than that of N-CQDs, and the accumulated holes on N-CQDs react with H2O or OH− to yield OH·. Finally, OH· with strong oxidative property can oxidize MB and remove it from aqueous solutions. Additionally, The EVB of TiO2 (2.72 eV vs NHE) is more positive than the standard redox potential of OH·/OH− (1.99 eV vs. NHE) [54], indicating that photoinduced holes from the VB of TiO2 can oxidize OH− to produce OH·. At the same time, the ECB (−0.52 eV) of TiO2 is more negative than the standard redox potential of O2/O2 − (−0.046 eV vs NHE) [55], implying that photoinduced electrons from the CB of TiO2 also can reduce O2 to produce O2 −. To further illustrate the photodegradation mechanism, the degradation process of organic pollutants can be described as follows:
(8) N-CQDs/TiO2 + hν → N-CQDs (h+) + TiO2 (e−)
(9) TiO2 (e−) + O2 → O2 −
(10) O2 − + H2O → H2O2 + OH·
(11) N-CQDs (h+) + H2O/OH− → OH·
(12) OH· + organic pollutants → CO2 +H2O

The above analysis is consistent with the result of in-situ capture techniques performed for determining the main active species in photocatalysis process that both the OH· and O2 − dominate the photodegradation of organics.

The photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs was further investigated by testing their ability to photocatalytically split water without the introduction of sacrificial agents or co-catalysts. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the average H2 evolution rates of prepared samples are 7.12, 17.75, 30.12, 12.67 and 9.81 μmol cm−2 h−1 corresponding to pure TiO2 NTAs, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-2, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-5 and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-7, respectively. Synchronously, O2 evolution can be detected during this photocatalytic process, and the average molar ratio of H2 to O2 evolution approaches 2, which is the theoretical value for overall water splitting. The above results reveal that the addition of N-CQDs to TiO2 NTAs significantly enhances their photocatalytic ability to split water, making them effective options for photocatalysis. Evidently, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 shows the best water splitting ability, which is also superior to TiO2 NTAs loaded with carbon dots [28,56]. To test their stability, N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 were used as water splitting photocatalysts for 5 consecutive cycling runs under simulated solar light irradiation. As expected, the gas was generated with only a slight decline after five successive cycles (Fig. 9b), confirming the outstanding photocatalytic stability of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs.

To further illustrate ability of N-CQDs to sensitize TiO2 NTAs, H2 evolution performance was conducted on pure TiO2 NTAs and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs under UV and visible light irradiation, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9c, after 5 consecutive cycling runs under UV light irradiation the H2 evolution rates of pristine TiO2 NTAs and N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 remained 6.33 and 21.26 μmol cm−2 h−1, respectively. The H2 evolution rate of the pristine TiO2 NTAs was almost constant under both UV light and full spectral irradiation, while the H2 evolution rate of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 was evidently improved under full spectral irradiation, confirming that N-CQDs are effective photosensitizing agents. This result is consistent with investigations of photoelectrochemical activity and IPCE spectra. Furthermore, the N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs-3 have an H2 generation rate of 4.77 μmol cm−2 h−1 after 5 consecutive cycling runs under visible light irradiation, while pristine TiO2 NTAs show nearly no H2 generation under the same conditions (Fig. 9d). Thus, it can be concluded that the optimum N-CQDs loaded on TiO2 NTAs effectively contribute to visible light photocatalytic H2 generation, due to the up-conversion or narrowing bandgap capability of N-CQDs to enormously boost visible light harvest.

A possible charge transfer mechanism for N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs is proposed based on the above investigations. As depicted in Scheme 1 , photogenerated electrons from the CB of N-CQDs can easily move to the CB of TiO2 due to favorable band alignment and potential difference under solar light irradiation. These electrons then transport along the highly ordered tubular structure of the Ti substrate, resulting in the significantly improved water reduction for H2 generation. Simultaneously, photogenerated holes from the VB of TiO2 can effectively move to the VB of N-CQDs, and these holes can oxidize water to produce O2. This efficient charge mobility leads to significantly enhanced photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic activity. Additionally, the prepared N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs show an extended light response range, which may be ascribed to the narrow bandgap of this heterostructure compared to pristine TiO2 NTAs. This narrow bandgap may result from the existence of electronic and chemical coupling between the CB of TiO2 and π states of the N-CQDs, or introducing new carbon energy levels into the TiO2 band that effectively narrows its bandgap [57]. The up-conversion property of N-CQDs can contribute to extended light harvest range due to its capability for transforming the long wavelength irradiation light into shorter wavelength emission light that can be utilized by TiO2 [58]. Furthermore, N-CQDs can be employed as electron containers to capture photogenerated electrons from TiO2 under solar light irradiation, further suppressing the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Therefore, the existence of N-CQDs can effectively promote photogenerated electrons separation and simultaneously restrain the recombination of electron-hole pairs, resulting in the significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs during photodegradation process of organic contaminants and in H2 generation from photocatalytic water splitting.

In summary, uniform, silk fibroin-derived N-CQDs were successfully anchored onto TiO2 NTAs to yield heterostructures via a facile hydrothermal process. When compared to pristine TiO2 NTAs, the heterostructures demonstrated vastly improved photocatalytic performance. They were found to effectively degrade organic contaminants and split water for H2 production without the addition of sacrificial agents or co-catalysts under simulated solar light irradiation. Additionally, the nanostructured photocatalyst manifested considerable cycling stability during continuous photocatalytic processes. The enhanced photocatalytic capability can be attributed to the formation of a heterostructure between the N-CQDs and TiO2 NTAs, which renders efficient transfer and separation of photoexcited charge carriers. This study provides insight into the rational design of highly efficient, low-cost, and sustainable photocatalysts. Moreover, it offers an intriguing TiO2 based photocatalyst that could potentially function as a promising candidate for water purification and hydrogen generation. Going beyond, we expect that other nanomaterials can also be integrated with TiO2-based catalysts to overcome its inherent shortfalls (i.e., poor light harvesting and conversion efficiency) and capitalize on its numerous promising attributes (e.g., low cost, sustainability, non-toxicity, easy to be engineered into various nanostructures). Finally, such appealing heterojunction also represents a potentially efficient multifunctional nanostructured catalyst with applications in energy and environmental fields (e.g., photoelectrochemical energy conversion and storage) and environmental remediation (e.g., water separation, purification, and disinfection).

The fluorescent N-CQDs with uniform size were prepared via a hydrothermal method using silk fibroin as a precursor. In detail, bombyx mori cocoons were boiled for 50 min in a 0.02 M NaHCO3 solution to remove the sericin, followed by washing in deionized water before drying in air. The degummed silk was then dissolved in a 10 M LiBr solution at 60 °C for 1h. The obtained silk fibroin solution was then filtered to remove impurities, followed by dialyzing in a dialysis bag (Silde-a-Lyzer dialysis cassettes, MWCO 3500) containing deionized water for 3 days at ambient temperature to remove salt. A centrifugal process was then performed on the dialyzed silk fibroin solution to remove silk aggregates. Various concentrations of silk fibroin solution (2, 3, 5 and 7 wt%) were obtained. Then, the silk fibroin solutions with different mass fractions underwent hydrothermal treatment for 12 h in an electric oven set to 220 °C. The obtained solutions were filtered prior to centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 30 min to remove impurities, followed by transferring into a high-speed centrifuge for centrifuging at 30000 rpm for 30 min to remove bulky grains. After centrifugation, the solution is clean N-CQDs aqueous solution.

A two-step anodization process was used to produce TiO2 NTAs. Before the first anodization, Ti foils were degreasing by ultrasonic cleaning and then dried at ambient temperature. For the anodization process, an ethylene glycol solution containing 0.5 wt% NH4F and 2 vol% H2O was used as an electrolyte, and Ti foil and Pt foil were employed as the anode and cathode, respectively. The oxide layer formed from Ti foil anodized with a DC power supply of 50 V for 2 h was removed by sonication in ethanol for the first anodization process, and then the treated Ti foil was anodized again at 50 V for 10 min to form nanoring-nanotube structures. These structures were then cleaned with deionized water and dried in a nitrogen atmosphere at ambient temperature for 30 min. The TiO2 NTAs were then annealed at 450 °C for 2 h (with the heating rate was controlled at 2 °C · min−1) to produce anatase TiO2. The annealed TiO2 NTAs and N-CQDs aqueous solution were then added into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and placed in an electric oven at 220 °C for 6 h, followed by vigorous washing with deionized water and drying in a nitrogen atmosphere at ambient temperature. The hydrothermal reaction leads to the direct anchoring of N-CQDs on internal and external surfaces of TiO2 NTAs, resulting in N-CQDs@TiO2 NTAs heterostructures.

SEM (Hitachi-S4800, Japan), TEM (FEI Tecnai G-20, USA) and HRTEM (FEI Tecnai F-20, USA) were used to measure the morphologies of prepared photocatalysts. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was employed to confirm the element compositions of prepared samples. AFM (Agilent 5500, USA) was employed to analyze the structure, morphology, and height of prepared N-CQDs. XRD (X'pert-Pro MRD, PANalytical, Holland) with Cu Kα radiation was employed to characterize crystalline structures of prepared samples. XPS recorded by a PHI 5600 spectrometer (Axis Ultra HAS, KRATOS, Japan) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source was used to analyze the chemical states of the prepared samples. The binding energies were standardized to C 1s signal at 284.8 eV. The Raman spectra used for analyzing structures of prepared samples were recorded by a Raman spectrometer (FM4P-TCSPC, HORIBA Jobin Yvon, UK) with an air-argon ion laser as excitation source (λ ex = 532 nm). FTIR (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Inc., USA) was employed to analyze the functional groups contained in prepared samples. TGA was performed using TA Instruments Thermogravimetric Analyzer (TGA/DSC2, METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland) at a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1 from room temperature to 800 °C under a purified air atmosphere. UV–vis spectrophotometry (Cary 5000, Agilent, USA) was used to record the UV–vis spectra of prepared samples. An FLS 980 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) was used to record PL, PLE and TRPL spectra of prepared samples, and a Xe lamp installed in the fluorescence spectrophotometer was employed as the excitation source (λ
ex = 375 nm).

The photoelectrochemical performances were conducted on prepared samples using an electrochemical workstation (PGSTAT302 N, Autolab, Switzerland) equipped with a standard three electrode configuration composed of a prepared sample (1 × 1 cm2), Pt foil, and an Ag/AgCl electrode serving as the working, counter and reference electrode, respectively. Ar was bubbled into a Na2SO4 solution (0.1 M, pH 6.0) for 30 min to purify electrolyte before performing photoelectrochemical measurements. A 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Trusttech Co. Ltd., China) was employed to provide the simulated sunlight with an intensity of 100 mW cm−2. The obtained UV and visible light from simulated sunlight were realized by presenting an optical filter in front of Xe lamp, allowing the passage of light with wavelength shorter and longer than 420 nm, respectively. LSV curves ranging from −0.5 V to 1.0 V vs Ag/AgCl were obtained from the electrochemical workstation with a scanning rate of 20 mV s−1. I-t curves were recorded at a bias potential of 0.3 V vs Ag/AgCl under chopped light illumination for 330 s with light on/off cycles for 30 s. EIS was recorded under a frequency range of 0.1–105 Hz with AC voltage amplitude of 5 mV at a bias of 0 V vs Ag/AgCl in 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution.

The photocatalytic activity measurement was performed on prepared photocatalysts by degrading MB in aqueous solution, with the initial concentration of MB aqueous solution being 10 mg L−1. The photodegradation process of MB was performed in quartz reactors under the same light source as photoelectrochemical measurements. Prior to providing light irradiation, the prepared photocatalysts (1.5 × 3.0 cm2), together with 10 mL of MB aqueous solution, were transferred into a quartz reactor for 30 min to equilibrate the MB adsorbed by photocatalysts. 3 mL of the MB aqueous solution sample was used to analyze the residual MB at given time intervals, and its concentration was calculated by recording the absorbance at the characteristic band of 664 nm in UV–vis absorption spectra with wavelength ranging from 300 to 800 nm. TOC was measured for confirming the photocatalytic capacity of the photocatalysts for degradation of organic pollutants. The TOC for MB degradation was detected by a TOC analyzer (TOC-VCPH, Shimadzu, Japan).

To further extend the application of prepared photocatalysts, photocatalytic H2 production experiments were conducted in a photoreactor at atmospheric pressure and ambient temperature. A 300 W Xe lamp used to simulate sunlight was and sat 20 cm from photocatalytic reactor. The prepared photocatalysts (1.5 × 3.0 cm2) together with 20 mL of deionized water were transferred into a quartz bottle placed in photoreactor, and then Ar was bubbled into the deionized water for 30 min to remove dissolved oxygen, ensuring an oxygen-free condition in the reaction system. The generated H2 and O2 signals were detected using gas chromatography (GC-7900, Sky Scientific Instrument Co. Ltd., China, Ar as a carrier gas, and 5 Å molecular sieve column). Every experiment was repeated three times to ensure the relative error of experimental data lower than 10%.

Qun Wang, Jianying Huang, and Yuekun Lai proposed and designed the research. Qun Wang and Jingsheng Cai performed the experiment and prepared the manuscript. Gill V. Biesold-McGee, Yun Hau Ng, Hongtao Sun, Jiping Wang, Yuekun Lai and Zhiqun Lin revised the manuscript. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qun Wang currently works at Shanghai University of Engineering Science. He received his Ph.D. degree from College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University in 2018. During his study on doctorate, he went to The University of New South Wales (UNSW Sydney) for doctoral joint training. His research interests focus on functional materials for environmental and energy applications.

Jingsheng Cai received his M.S. degree from Soochow University of College of Textile and Clothing Engineering in 2018. He is currently a Ph.D. candidate in College of Energy, Soochow Institute for Energy and Materials InnovationS (SIEMIS), Soochow University under the supervision of Prof. Zhongfan Liu and Prof. Jingyu Sun. His current research focuses 3D-printed energy storage devices.

Gill Biesold-McGee received his Bachelor of Science in Materials Science and Engineering at Clemson University in 2017. He is currently a PhD student in the School of Materials Science and Engineering at Georgia Institute of Technology. His research interests include exploring the luminescent properties of semiconductor nanocrystals in a variety of environments.

Jianying Huang received Ph.D. degree in 2007 from the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University. During 2007–2011, she is an Assistant Professor at Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter. After that, she acted as visiting scholar at Muenster University. From 2013, she was an Associate Professor at School of Textile and Clothing Engineering in Soochow University. Currently, she is a Full Professor at the College of Chemical Engineering at Fuzhou University, and selected as the 2019 Highly Cited Researchers (Clarivate Analytics). Her research interests focus on the bio-inspired intelligent textile surfaces with special wettability, ATRP and click-chemistry reaction.

Yun Hau Ng is an associate professor in the School of Energy and Environment, City University of Hong Kong. He received his PhD from Osaka University in 2009. Before joining City University of Hong Kong, he was a lecturer (2014) and senior lecturer (2016) in the School of Chemical Engineering, University of New South Wales. His research is focused on the development of novel photoactive semiconductors for sunlight energy conversion. He received the APEC ASPIRE Prize in 2019, Distinguished Lectureship Award from the Chemical Society of Japan in 2018, and Honda-Fujishima Prize by the Electrochemical Society of Japan in 2013.

Hongtao Sun is currently a principal researcher at International Center for Materials Nanoarchitectonics (MANA), National Institute for Materials Science (NIMS), Japan. He received his Ph.D. degree in materials science from Chinese Academy of Sciences. From 2012 to 2013, he was an assistant professor at Faculty of Engineering, Hokkaido University, Japan. From 2013 to 2020, he was a professor at College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, China. His current research topics are emerging optoelectronic materials including halide perovskites and UVC persistent phosphors, and their applications for LEDs, solar cells and biomedicine.

Jiping Wang is currently a national distinguished professor and vice chair of the academic committee at Shanghai University of Engineering Science. He also serves as an AATCC (American Association of Textile Chemists and Colorists) board member and publication committee chair; and an associate editor of Journal of Surfactants and Detergents. With more than 80 granted patents and 140 scientific publications, he has profound knowledge and skills in textile chemistry and advanced materials. He was awarded “The Science Chinese Annual Figure” in 2014, “International Association of Advanced Materials (IAAM) Medal in 2017 and IAAM Fellow in 2020.

Dr. Yuekun Lai is a Fujian province “Minjiang Scholar” chair professor at the College of Chemical Engineering at Fuzhou University, China. He received his Ph.D. degree from the Department of Chemistry, Xiamen University. He is selected as the 2018, 2019 Highly Cited Researchers (Clarivate Analytics) and the recipient of the 2016 Journal of Materials Chemistry A (JMCA, RSC) Emerging Investigators and 2019 Advanced Materials Interfaces (AMI, Wiley) Hall of Fame award. His current research topics are bioinspired intelligent materials with special wettability, multifunctional fabrics, and functional nanostructures for energy and environmental applications.

Zhiqun Lin is a Professor in the School of Materials Science and Engineering at the Georgia Institute of Technology. His research interests include batteries, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, solar cells, semiconductor organic-inorganic nanocomposites, multifunctional nanocrystals, block copolymers, hierarchical structure formation and assembly, and surface and interfacial properties.
